1	------------------------ MODULE FunctionTheorems ----------------------------
2	(***************************************************************************)
3	(* `^{\large \vspace{12pt}                                                 *)
4	(*  Proofs of facts about functions.                                       *)
5	(*  Originally contributed by Tom Rodeheffer, MSR.                         *)
6	(*  \vspace{12pt}}^'                                                       *)
7	(***************************************************************************)
8	
9	EXTENDS Functions, Integers, FiniteSets
10	
11	(***************************************************************************)
12	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
13	(*                                                                         *)
14	(* Function restriction.                                                   *)
15	(*                                                                         *)
16	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
17	(***************************************************************************)
18	THEOREM Fun_RestrictProperties ==
19	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in [S -> T], NEW A \in SUBSET S
20	  PROVE  /\ Restrict(f,A) \in [A -> T]
21	         /\ \A x \in A : Restrict(f,A)[x] = f[x]
22	
23	(***************************************************************************)
24	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
25	(*                                                                         *)
26	(* Range of a function.                                                    *)
27	(*                                                                         *)
28	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
29	(***************************************************************************)
30	THEOREM Fun_RangeProperties ==
31	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in [S -> T]
32	  PROVE  /\ Range(f) \subseteq T
33	         /\ \A y \in Range(f) : \E x \in S : f[x] = y
34	         /\ f \in Surjection(S, Range(f))
35	
36	
37	(***************************************************************************)
38	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
39	(*                                                                         *)
40	(* Inverse of a function.                                                  *)
41	(*                                                                         *)
42	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
43	(***************************************************************************)
44	THEOREM Fun_InverseProperties ==
45	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in [S -> T]
46	  PROVE  /\ (S = {} => T = {}) => Inverse(f,S,T) \in [T -> S]
47	         /\ \A y \in Range(f) : f[Inverse(f,S,T)[y]] = y
48	
49	
50	(***************************************************************************)
51	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
52	(*                                                                         *)
53	(* Introduction rules for injections, surjections, bijections.             *)
54	(*                                                                         *)
55	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
56	(***************************************************************************)
57	THEOREM Fun_IsInj ==
58	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW F \in [S -> T],
59	         \A a,b \in S : F[a] = F[b] => a = b
60	  PROVE  F \in Injection(S,T)
61	
62	THEOREM Fun_IsSurj ==
63	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW F \in [S -> T],
64	         \A t \in T : \E s \in S : F[s] = t
65	  PROVE  F \in Surjection(S,T)
66	
67	THEOREM Fun_IsBij ==
68	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW F,
69	         \/ F \in Injection(S,T)
70	         \/ (F \in [S -> T] /\ \A a,b \in S : F[a] = F[b] => a = b),
71	
72	         \/ F \in Surjection(S,T)
73	         \/ (F \in [S -> T] /\ \A t \in T : \E s \in S : F[s] = t)
74	  PROVE  F \in Bijection(S,T)
75	
76	
77	
78	(***************************************************************************)
79	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
80	(*                                                                         *)
81	(* Properties of injections, surjections, and bijections.                  *)
82	(*                                                                         *)
83	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
84	(***************************************************************************)
85	THEOREM Fun_InjectionProperties ==
86	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW F \in Injection(S,T)
87	  PROVE  /\ F \in [S -> T]
88	         /\ \A a,b \in S : F[a] = F[b] => a = b
89	
90	THEOREM Fun_SurjectionProperties ==
91	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW F \in Surjection(S,T)
92	  PROVE  /\ F \in [S -> T]
93	         /\ \A t \in T : \E s \in S : F[s] = t
94	         /\ Range(F) = T
95	
96	THEOREM Fun_BijectionProperties ==
97	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW F \in Bijection(S,T)
98	  PROVE  /\ F \in [S -> T]
99	         /\ F \in Injection(S,T)
100	         /\ F \in Surjection(S,T)
101	         /\ \A a,b \in S : F[a] = F[b] => a = b
102	         /\ \A t \in T : \E s \in S : F[s] = t
103	
104	
105	(***************************************************************************)
106	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
107	(*                                                                         *)
108	(* A surjection in [S -> T] such that there is no surjection from any      *)
109	(* subset of S to T is a bijection.                                        *)
110	(*                                                                         *)
111	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
112	(***************************************************************************)
113	THEOREM Fun_SmallestSurjectionIsBijection ==
114	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in Surjection(S,T),
115	         \A U \in SUBSET S : U # S => Surjection(U,T) = {}
116	  PROVE  f \in Bijection(S,T)
117	
118	
119	(***************************************************************************)
120	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
121	(*                                                                         *)
122	(* Transitivity of injections, surjections, bijections.                    *)
123	(*                                                                         *)
124	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
125	(***************************************************************************)
126	THEOREM Fun_InjTransitive ==
127	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW U,
128	         NEW F \in Injection(S,T),
129	         NEW G \in Injection(T,U)
130	  PROVE  [s \in S |-> G[F[s]]] \in Injection(S,U)
131	
132	THEOREM Fun_SurjTransitive ==
133	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW U,
134	         NEW F \in Surjection(S,T),
135	         NEW G \in Surjection(T,U)
136	  PROVE  [s \in S |-> G[F[s]]] \in Surjection(S,U)
137	
138	THEOREM Fun_BijTransitive ==
139	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW U,
140	         NEW F \in Bijection(S,T),
141	         NEW G \in Bijection(T,U)
142	  PROVE  [s \in S |-> G[F[s]]] \in Bijection(S,U)
143	
144	
145	(***************************************************************************)
146	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
147	(*                                                                         *)
148	(* The inverse of a surjection is an injection and vice versa.             *)
149	(*                                                                         *)
150	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
151	(***************************************************************************)
152	THEOREM Fun_SurjInverse ==
153	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in Surjection(S,T)
154	  PROVE  Inverse(f,S,T) \in Injection(T,S)
155	
156	THEOREM Fun_InjInverse ==
157	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in Injection(S,T), S = {} => T = {}
158	  PROVE  Inverse(f,S,T) \in Surjection(T,S)
159	
160	
161	(***************************************************************************)
162	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
163	(*                                                                         *)
164	(* Properties of the inverse of a bijection.                               *)
165	(*                                                                         *)
166	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
167	(***************************************************************************)
168	THEOREM Fun_BijInverse ==
169	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in Bijection(S,T)
170	  PROVE  /\ Inverse(f,S,T) \in Bijection(T,S)
171	         /\ \A s \in S : Inverse(f,S,T)[f[s]] = s
172	         /\ Inverse(Inverse(f,S,T), T,S) = f
173	
174	(***************************************************************************)
175	(* We cannot conclude that a function f is a bijection if its inverse is   *)
176	(* a bijection, and the following is a counter-example.                    *)
177	(*                                                                         *)
178	(*   S = {1,2} T = {3,4}                                                   *)
179	(*   f = [x \in S |-> 3]                                                   *)
180	(*                                                                         *)
181	(* Then, Inverse(f,S,T) can very well be (3 :> 1) @@ (4 :> 2), which is a  *)
182	(* bijection although f is not.                                            *)
183	(***************************************************************************)
184	
185	(***************************************************************************)
186	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
187	(*                                                                         *)
188	(* The restriction of a bijection is a bijection.                          *)
189	(*                                                                         *)
190	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
191	(***************************************************************************)
192	THEOREM Fun_BijRestrict ==
193	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW F \in Bijection(S,T),
194	         NEW R \in SUBSET S
195	  PROVE  Restrict(F, R) \in Bijection(R, Range(Restrict(F, R)))
196	
197	
198	(***************************************************************************)
199	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
200	(*                                                                         *)
201	(* Given F an injection from S to T, then F is a bijection from S to F(S). *)
202	(*                                                                         *)
203	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
204	(***************************************************************************)
205	THEOREM Fun_InjMeansBijImage ==
206	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW F \in Injection(S,T)
207	  PROVE  F \in Bijection(S, Range(F))
208	
209	
210	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
211	(***************************************************************************)
212	(* `^{\large \vspace{12pt}                                                 *)
213	(*  Facts about exists jections.                                           *)
214	(*  \vspace{12pt}}^'                                                       *)
215	(***************************************************************************)
216	
217	
218	
219	(***************************************************************************)
220	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
221	(*                                                                         *)
222	(* Definitions restated as facts.                                          *)
223	(*                                                                         *)
224	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
225	(***************************************************************************)
226	THEOREM Fun_ExistsInj ==
227	  \A S,T : ExistsInjection(S,T)  <=>  Injection(S,T) # {}
228	
229	THEOREM Fun_ExistsSurj ==
230	  \A S,T : ExistsSurjection(S,T)  <=>  Surjection(S,T) # {}
231	
232	THEOREM Fun_ExistsBij ==
233	  \A S,T : ExistsBijection(S,T)  <=>  Bijection(S,T) # {}
234	
235	
236	(***************************************************************************)
237	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
238	(*                                                                         *)
239	(* There is a surjection from any set S to any non-empty subset T of S.    *)
240	(* (Note that there cannot be a surjection to {} except if S is empty.)    *)
241	(*                                                                         *)
242	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
243	(***************************************************************************)
244	THEOREM Fun_ExistsSurjSubset ==
245	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T \in SUBSET S, T # {}
246	  PROVE  ExistsSurjection(S,T)
247	
248	
249	(***************************************************************************)
250	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
251	(*                                                                         *)
252	(* If there is a surjection from S to T, then there is an injection from T *)
253	(* to S.                                                                   *)
254	(*                                                                         *)
255	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
256	(***************************************************************************)
257	THEOREM Fun_ExistsSurjMeansExistsRevInj ==
258	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, ExistsSurjection(S,T)
259	  PROVE  ExistsInjection(T,S)
260	
261	
262	(***************************************************************************)
263	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
264	(*                                                                         *)
265	(* ExistsBijection is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.                *)
266	(*                                                                         *)
267	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
268	(***************************************************************************)
269	THEOREM Fun_ExistsBijReflexive ==
270	  ASSUME NEW S
271	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(S,S)
272	
273	THEOREM Fun_ExistsBijSymmetric ==
274	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, ExistsBijection(S,T)
275	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(T,S)
276	
277	THEOREM Fun_ExistsBijTransitive ==
278	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW U, ExistsBijection(S,T), ExistsBijection(T,U)
279	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(S,U)
280	
281	
282	(***************************************************************************)
283	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
284	(*                                                                         *)
285	(* Existence of injections and surjections is reflexive and transitive.    *)
286	(*                                                                         *)
287	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
288	(***************************************************************************)
289	THEOREM Fun_ExistsInjReflexive ==
290	  ASSUME NEW S
291	  PROVE  ExistsInjection(S,S)
292	
293	THEOREM Fun_ExistsSurjReflexive ==
294	  ASSUME NEW S
295	  PROVE  ExistsSurjection(S,S)
296	
297	THEOREM Fun_ExistsInjTransitive ==
298	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW U,
299	         ExistsInjection(S,T), ExistsInjection(T,U)
300	  PROVE  ExistsInjection(S,U)
301	
302	THEOREM Fun_ExistsSurjTransitive ==
303	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW U,
304	         ExistsSurjection(S,T), ExistsSurjection(T,U)
305	  PROVE  ExistsSurjection(S,U)
306	
307	
308	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
309	(***************************************************************************)
310	(* `^{\large \vspace{12pt}                                                 *)
311	(* The Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem.                                 *)
312	(*  \vspace{12pt}}^'                                                       *)
313	(***************************************************************************)
314	
315	
316	(***************************************************************************)
317	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
318	(*                                                                         *)
319	(* If there exists an injection from S to T, where T is a subset of S,     *)
320	(* then there exists a bijection from S to T.                              *)
321	(*                                                                         *)
322	(* A lemma for the Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem.                     *)
323	(*                                                                         *)
324	(* This proof is formalized from                                           *)
325	(* `^\url{http://www.proofwiki.org/wiki/Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder\_Theorem/Lemma}^' *)
326	(* retrieved April 29, 2013.                                               *)
327	(*                                                                         *)
328	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
329	(***************************************************************************)
330	THEOREM Fun_CantorBernsteinSchroeder_Lemma ==
331	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, T \subseteq S, ExistsInjection(S,T)
332	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(S,T)
333	
334	
335	(***************************************************************************)
336	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
337	(*                                                                         *)
338	(* If an injection exists from S to T and an injection exists from T to S, *)
339	(* then there is a bijection from S to T.                                  *)
340	(*                                                                         *)
341	(* This is the Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem.                         *)
342	(*                                                                         *)
343	(* This proof is formalized from                                           *)
344	(* `^\url{http://www.proofwiki.org/wiki/Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder_Theorem/Proof_5}^' *)
345	(* retrieved April 29, 2013.                                               *)
346	(*                                                                         *)
347	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
348	(***************************************************************************)
349	THEOREM Fun_CantorBernsteinSchroeder ==
350	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T,
351	         ExistsInjection(S,T), ExistsInjection(T,S)
352	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(S,T)
353	
354	
355	(***************************************************************************)
356	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
357	(*                                                                         *)
358	(* Applications of the Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder Theorem.                 *)
359	(* If there exists an injection f: A->B and a surjection g: A->B, then     *)
360	(* there exists a bijection between A and B.                               *)
361	(* Also, if there are surjections between A and B, then there is a         *)
362	(* bijection.                                                              *)
363	(*                                                                         *)
364	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
365	(***************************************************************************)
366	
367	THEOREM Fun_ExistInjAndSurjThenBij ==
368	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T,
369	         ExistsInjection(S,T), ExistsSurjection(S,T)
370	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(S,T)
371	
372	THEOREM Fun_ExistSurjAndSurjThenBij ==
373	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T,
374	         ExistsSurjection(S,T), ExistsSurjection(T,S)
375	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(S,T)
376	
377	
378	(***************************************************************************)
379	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
380	(*                                                                         *)
381	(* Equivalences for ExistsBijection.                                       *)
382	(*                                                                         *)
383	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
384	(***************************************************************************)
385	THEOREM Fun_ExistsBijEquiv ==
386	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T
387	  PROVE  /\ ExistsBijection(S,T) <=> ExistsBijection(T,S)
388	         /\ ExistsBijection(S,T) <=> ExistsInjection(S,T) /\ ExistsInjection(T,S)
389	         /\ ExistsBijection(S,T) <=> ExistsInjection(S,T) /\ ExistsSurjection(S,T)
390	         /\ ExistsBijection(S,T) <=> ExistsInjection(T,S) /\ ExistsSurjection(T,S)
391	         /\ ExistsBijection(S,T) <=> ExistsSurjection(S,T) /\ ExistsSurjection(T,S)
392	
393	
394	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
395	(***************************************************************************)
396	(* `^{\large \vspace{12pt}                                                 *)
397	(*  Facts about jections involving 1..n.                                   *)
398	(*  \vspace{12pt}}^'                                                       *)
399	(***************************************************************************)
400	
401	
402	(***************************************************************************)
403	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
404	(*                                                                         *)
405	(* There is a bijection from 1..b-a+1 to a..b for integers a,b with a <= b.*)
406	(*                                                                         *)
407	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
408	(***************************************************************************)
409	THEOREM Fun_ExistsBijInterval ==
410	  ASSUME NEW a \in Int, NEW b \in Int, a <= b
411	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(1 .. b-a+1, a .. b)
412	
413	
414	(***************************************************************************)
415	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
416	(*                                                                         *)
417	(* There is an injection from 1..n to 1..m iff n \leq m.                   *)
418	(*                                                                         *)
419	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
420	(***************************************************************************)
421	THEOREM Fun_NatInjLeq ==
422	  ASSUME NEW n \in Nat, NEW m \in Nat
423	  PROVE  ExistsInjection(1..n,1..m) <=> n \leq m
424	
425	
426	(***************************************************************************)
427	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
428	(*                                                                         *)
429	(* If a surjection from 1..n to S exists (for some n \in Nat) then a       *)
430	(* bijection from 1..m to S exists (for some m \in Nat) and m \leq n.      *)
431	(*                                                                         *)
432	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
433	(***************************************************************************)
434	THEOREM Fun_NatSurjImpliesNatBij ==
435	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW n \in Nat, ExistsSurjection(1..n,S)
436	  PROVE  \E m \in Nat : ExistsBijection(1..m,S) /\ m \leq n
437	
438	
439	(***************************************************************************)
440	(* Simple corollary.                                                       *)
441	(***************************************************************************)
442	THEOREM Fun_NatSurjEquivNatBij ==
443	  ASSUME NEW S
444	  PROVE  (\E n \in Nat : ExistsSurjection(1..n,S))
445	    <=>  (\E m \in Nat : ExistsBijection(1..m,S))
446	
447	
448	(***************************************************************************)
449	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
450	(*                                                                         *)
451	(* For any set S, given n, m \in Nat such that bijections exist from 1..n  *)
452	(* to S and from 1..m to S, then it must be the case that n = m.           *)
453	(*                                                                         *)
454	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
455	(***************************************************************************)
456	THEOREM Fun_NatBijSame ==
457	  ASSUME NEW S,
458	         NEW n \in Nat, ExistsBijection(1..n,S),
459	         NEW m \in Nat, ExistsBijection(1..m,S)
460	  PROVE  n = m
461	
462	
463	(***************************************************************************)
464	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
465	(*                                                                         *)
466	(* S is empty iff there exists a bijection from 1..0 to S.                 *)
467	(*                                                                         *)
468	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
469	(***************************************************************************)
470	THEOREM Fun_NatBijEmpty ==
471	  ASSUME NEW S
472	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(1..0,S) <=> S = {}
473	
474	
475	(***************************************************************************)
476	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
477	(*                                                                         *)
478	(* S is a singleton iff there exists a bijection from 1..1 to S.           *)
479	(*                                                                         *)
480	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
481	(***************************************************************************)
482	THEOREM Fun_NatBijSingleton ==
483	  ASSUME NEW S
484	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(1..1,S) <=> \E s : S = {s}
485	
486	
487	(***************************************************************************)
488	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
489	(*                                                                         *)
490	(* If there exists a bijection from 1..m to S (for some m \in Nat), then   *)
491	(* there exists a bijection from 1..n to T (for some n \in Nat), where T   *)
492	(* is a subset of S.  Furthermore n \leq m.                                *)
493	(*                                                                         *)
494	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
495	(***************************************************************************)
496	THEOREM Fun_NatBijSubset ==
497	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW m \in Nat, ExistsBijection(1..m,S),
498	         NEW T \in SUBSET S
499	  PROVE  \E n \in Nat : ExistsBijection(1..n,T) /\ n \leq m
500	
501	
502	(***************************************************************************)
503	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
504	(*                                                                         *)
505	(* If there exists a bijection from 1..m to S (for some m \in Nat), then   *)
506	(* there exists a bijection from 1..(m+1) to S \cup {x}, where x \notin S. *)
507	(*                                                                         *)
508	(*                                                                         *)
509	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
510	(***************************************************************************)
511	THEOREM Fun_NatBijAddElem ==
512	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW m \in Nat, ExistsBijection(1..m,S),
513	         NEW x, x \notin S
514	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(1..(m+1), S \cup {x})
515	
516	
517	(***************************************************************************)
518	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
519	(*                                                                         *)
520	(* If there exists a bijection from 1..m to S (for some m \in Nat), then   *)
521	(* there exists a bijection from 1..(m-1) to S \ {x}, where x \in S.       *)
522	(*                                                                         *)
523	(*                                                                         *)
524	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
525	(***************************************************************************)
526	THEOREM Fun_NatBijSubElem ==
527	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW m \in Nat, ExistsBijection(1..m,S),
528	         NEW x, x \in S
529	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(1..(m-1), S \ {x})
530	
531	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
532	
533	(***************************************************************************)
534	(* Folding a function for an empty set of indices yields the base value.   *)
535	(***************************************************************************)
536	THEOREM FoldFunctionOnSetEmpty ==
537	  ASSUME NEW op(_,_), NEW base, NEW fun
538	  PROVE  FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, {}) = base 
539	
540	(***************************************************************************)
541	(* Folding a function for a non-empty set of indices corresponds to        *)
542	(* applying the binary operator to f[i], for some i \in indices, and the   *)
543	(* result of recursing for the set indices \ {i}.                          *)
544	(***************************************************************************)
545	THEOREM FoldFunctionOnSetNonempty ==
546	  ASSUME NEW op(_,_), NEW base, NEW fun, 
547	         NEW indices, indices # {}, IsFiniteSet(indices)
548	  PROVE  \E i \in indices : FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices) = 
549	            op(fun[i], FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices \ {i}))
550	
551	(***************************************************************************)
552	(* The type of folding a function corresponds to the type associated with  *)
553	(* the binary operator that underlies the fold.                            *)
554	(***************************************************************************)
555	THEOREM FoldFunctionOnSetType ==
556	  ASSUME NEW Typ, NEW op(_,_), NEW base \in Typ,
557	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) \in Typ,
558	         NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices), NEW fun,
559	         \A i \in indices : fun[i] \in Typ
560	  PROVE FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices) \in Typ
561	
562	(***************************************************************************)
563	(* Folding two functions that agree on every element of the set of indices *)
564	(* yields the same value.                                                  *)
565	(***************************************************************************)
566	THEOREM FoldFunctionOnSetEqual ==
567	  ASSUME NEW op(_,_), NEW base, NEW f, NEW g,
568	         NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices),
569	         \A i \in indices : f[i] = g[i]
570	  PROVE  FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, f, indices) = 
571	         FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, g, indices)
572	
573	(***************************************************************************)
574	(* If the binary operator is associative and commutative, the result of    *)
575	(* folding a function over a non-empty set corresponds to applying the     *)
576	(* operator to an arbitrary element of the set and the result of folding   *)
577	(* the function over the remainder of the set.                             *)
578	(***************************************************************************)
579	THEOREM FoldFunctionOnSetAC ==
580	  ASSUME NEW Typ, NEW op(_,_), NEW base \in Typ,
581	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) \in Typ,
582	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) = op(u,t),
583	         \A t,u,v \in Typ : op(t, op(u,v)) = op(op(t,u),v),
584	         NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices), NEW i \in indices, NEW fun,
585	         \A j \in indices : fun[j] \in Typ
586	  PROVE FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices) =
587	        op(fun[i], FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices \ {i}))
588	
589	\* reformulation in terms of adding an index to the set
590	THEOREM FoldFunctionOnSetACAddIndex ==
591	  ASSUME NEW Typ, NEW op(_,_), NEW base \in Typ,
592	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) \in Typ,
593	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) = op(u,t),
594	         \A t,u,v \in Typ : op(t, op(u,v)) = op(op(t,u),v),
595	         NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices), NEW i, i \notin indices,
596	         NEW fun, \A j \in indices \union {i} : fun[j] \in Typ
597	  PROVE FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices \union {i}) =
598	        op(fun[i], FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices))
599	
600	(*************************************************************************)
601	(* For an AC operator, folding an EXCEPT construction can be reduced to  *)
602	(* folding the original function.                                        *)
603	(*************************************************************************)
604	THEOREM FoldFunctionOnSetACExcept ==
605	  ASSUME NEW Typ, NEW op(_,_), NEW base \in Typ,
606	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) \in Typ,
607	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) = op(u,t),
608	         \A t,u,v \in Typ : op(t, op(u,v)) = op(op(t,u),v),
609	         NEW fun, NEW i, NEW y \in Typ,
610	         NEW indices \in SUBSET (DOMAIN fun), IsFiniteSet(indices), 
611	         \A j \in indices : fun[j] \in Typ 
612	  PROVE  FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, [fun EXCEPT ![i] = y], indices) =
613	         IF i \in indices
614	         THEN op(y, FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices \ {i}))
615	         ELSE FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, indices)
616	
617	(*************************************************************************)
618	(* For an AC operator `op` for which `base` is the neutral element,      *)
619	(* folding a function commutes with set union, for disjoint sets.        *)
620	(*************************************************************************)
621	THEOREM FoldFunctionOnSetDisjointUnion ==
622	  ASSUME NEW Typ, NEW op(_,_), NEW base \in Typ,
623	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) \in Typ,
624	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) = op(u,t),
625	         \A t,u,v \in Typ : op(t, op(u,v)) = op(op(t,u),v),
626	         \A t \in Typ : op(base, t) = t,
627	         NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T), S \cap T = {},
628	         NEW fun, \A i \in S \union T : fun[i] \in Typ
629	  PROVE FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, S \union T) =
630	        op(FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, S), FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, T))
631	
632	(*************************************************************************)
633	(* Analogous theorems for FoldFunction. Note that the theorems about     *)
634	(* adding an index and about disjoint union do not make sense here.      *)
635	(*************************************************************************)
636	THEOREM FoldFunctionEmpty ==
637	  ASSUME NEW op(_,_), NEW base, NEW fun, DOMAIN fun = {}
638	  PROVE  FoldFunction(op, base, fun) = base 
639	
640	THEOREM FoldFunctionNonempty ==
641	  ASSUME NEW op(_,_), NEW base, NEW fun, 
642	         DOMAIN fun # {}, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun)
643	  PROVE  \E i \in DOMAIN fun : FoldFunction(op, base, fun) = 
644	            op(fun[i], FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, DOMAIN fun \ {i}))
645	
646	THEOREM FoldFunctionType ==
647	  ASSUME NEW Typ, NEW op(_,_), NEW base \in Typ,
648	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) \in Typ,
649	         NEW fun, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun),
650	         \A i \in DOMAIN fun : fun[i] \in Typ
651	  PROVE FoldFunction(op, base, fun) \in Typ
652	
653	THEOREM FoldFunctionAC ==
654	  ASSUME NEW Typ, NEW op(_,_), NEW base \in Typ,
655	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) \in Typ,
656	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) = op(u,t),
657	         \A t,u,v \in Typ : op(t, op(u,v)) = op(op(t,u),v),
658	         NEW fun, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun), NEW i \in DOMAIN fun,
659	         \A j \in DOMAIN fun : fun[j] \in Typ
660	  PROVE FoldFunction(op, base, fun) =
661	        op(fun[i], FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, (DOMAIN fun) \ {i}))
662	
663	THEOREM FoldFunctionACExcept ==
664	  ASSUME NEW Typ, NEW op(_,_), NEW base \in Typ,
665	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) \in Typ,
666	         \A t,u \in Typ : op(t,u) = op(u,t),
667	         \A t,u,v \in Typ : op(t, op(u,v)) = op(op(t,u),v),
668	         NEW fun, NEW i, NEW y \in Typ,
669	         IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun), 
670	         \A j \in DOMAIN fun : fun[j] \in Typ 
671	  PROVE  FoldFunction(op, base, [fun EXCEPT ![i] = y]) =
672	         IF i \in DOMAIN fun
673	         THEN op(y, FoldFunctionOnSet(op, base, fun, (DOMAIN fun) \ {i}))
674	         ELSE FoldFunction(op, base, fun)
675	
676	(*************************************************************************)
677	(* Summing a function that returns natural numbers, resp. integers,      *)
678	(* yields a natural number, resp. an integer.                            *)
679	(*************************************************************************)
680	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetNat ==
681	  ASSUME NEW fun, NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices),
682	         \A i \in indices : fun[i] \in Nat 
683	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices) \in Nat
684	
685	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetInt ==
686	  ASSUME NEW fun, NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices),
687	         \A i \in indices : fun[i] \in Int
688	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices) \in Int
689	
690	(*************************************************************************)
691	(* Summing two functions that agree on all relevant indices yields the   *)
692	(* same result.                                                          *)
693	(*************************************************************************)
694	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetEqual ==
695	  ASSUME NEW f, NEW g, NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices),
696	         \A i \in indices : f[i] = g[i]
697	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(f, indices) = SumFunctionOnSet(g, indices)
698	
699	(*************************************************************************)
700	(* Summing a function over the empty set is 0.                           *)
701	(*************************************************************************)
702	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetEmpty ==
703	  ASSUME NEW fun 
704	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, {}) = 0
705	
706	(*************************************************************************)
707	(* Summing a function over a non-empty set corresponds to the sum of     *)
708	(* element of the set and the sum of the function over the remainder.    *)
709	(*************************************************************************)
710	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetNonempty ==
711	  ASSUME NEW fun, NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices), NEW i \in indices,
712	         \A j \in indices : fun[j] \in Int
713	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices) = 
714	         fun[i] + SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices \ {i})
715	
716	\* reformulation in terms of adding an index
717	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetAddIndex ==
718	  ASSUME NEW fun, NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices), 
719	         NEW i, i \notin indices,
720	         \A j \in indices \union {i} : fun[j] \in Int
721	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices \union {i}) = 
722	         fun[i] + SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices)
723	
724	\* corresponding lemma for removing an index
725	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetRemoveIndex ==
726	  ASSUME NEW fun, NEW indices, IsFiniteSet(indices), NEW i \in indices,
727	         \A j \in indices : fun[j] \in Int
728	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices \ {i}) = 
729	         SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices) - fun[i]
730	
731	(*************************************************************************)
732	(* Reduce the sum of an EXCEPT to the sum of the original function.      *)
733	(*************************************************************************)
734	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetExcept ==
735	  ASSUME NEW fun, NEW i, NEW y \in Int,
736	         NEW indices \in SUBSET (DOMAIN fun), IsFiniteSet(indices),
737	         \A j \in indices : fun[j] \in Int 
738	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet([fun EXCEPT ![i] = y], indices) =
739	         IF i \in indices
740	         THEN SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices) + y - fun[i]
741	         ELSE SumFunctionOnSet(fun, indices)
742	
743	(*************************************************************************)
744	(* Summing a function distributes over disjoint union.                   *)
745	(*************************************************************************)
746	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetDisjointUnion ==
747	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
748	         NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T), S \cap T = {},
749	         NEW fun, \A x \in S \union T : fun[x] \in Int 
750	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, S \union T) =
751	         SumFunctionOnSet(fun, S) + SumFunctionOnSet(fun, T)
752	
753	(*************************************************************************)
754	(* Summing a Nat-valued function is monotonic in the subset relation.    *)
755	(*************************************************************************)
756	THEOREM SumFunctionNatOnSubset ==
757	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW T \in SUBSET S,
758	         NEW fun, \A s \in S : fun[s] \in Nat
759	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, T) <= SumFunctionOnSet(fun, S)
760	
761	(*************************************************************************)
762	(* The sum of a Nat-valued function is zero iff all relevant function    *)
763	(* values are zero.                                                      *)
764	(*************************************************************************)
765	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetZero ==
766	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
767	         NEW fun, \A x \in S : fun[x] \in Nat
768	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(fun, S) = 0 <=> \A x \in S : fun[x] = 0
769	
770	(*************************************************************************)
771	(* Given any Int-valued function f and finite set S such that f[s] = c   *)
772	(* for all s \in S, summing f over S yields c multiplied by the          *)
773	(* cardinality of S. For example, for                                    *)
774	(*   f == [s \in {"a", "b", "c"} |-> IF s = "c" THEN 42 ELSE 5],         *)
775	(*   SumFunctionOnSet(f, {"a","b"}) = 10.                                *)
776	(*************************************************************************)
777	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetConst ==
778	  ASSUME NEW D, NEW S \in SUBSET D, IsFiniteSet(S),
779	         NEW f \in [D -> Int], NEW c \in Int, \A x \in S : f[x] = c 
780	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(f, S) = c * Cardinality(S)
781	
782	(*************************************************************************)
783	(* Summing a function is monotonic in the function argument.             *)
784	(*************************************************************************)
785	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetMonotonic ==
786	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
787	         NEW f, \A s \in S : f[s] \in Int,
788	         NEW g, \A s \in S : g[s] \in Int,
789	         \A s \in S : f[s] <= g[s]
790	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(f, S) <= SumFunctionOnSet(g, S)
791	
792	(*************************************************************************)
793	(* The sum over function f is strictly smaller than the sum over g if    *)
794	(* f[x] <= g[x] for all x \in S and f[x] < g[s] holds for at least some  *)
795	(* element s \in S.                                                      *)
796	(*************************************************************************)
797	THEOREM SumFunctionOnSetStrictlyMonotonic ==
798	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
799	         NEW f, \A s \in S : f[s] \in Int,
800	         NEW g, \A s \in S : g[s] \in Int,
801	         \A x \in S : f[x] <= g[x],
802	         NEW s \in S, f[s] < g[s]
803	  PROVE  SumFunctionOnSet(f, S) < SumFunctionOnSet(g, S)
804	
805	(*************************************************************************)
806	(* Similar theorems for SumFunction.                                     *)
807	(*************************************************************************)
808	THEOREM SumFunctionNat ==
809	  ASSUME NEW fun, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun),
810	         \A x \in DOMAIN fun : fun[x] \in Nat 
811	  PROVE  SumFunction(fun) \in Nat
812	
813	THEOREM SumFunctionInt ==
814	  ASSUME NEW fun, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun),
815	         \A x \in DOMAIN fun : fun[x] \in Int 
816	  PROVE  SumFunction(fun) \in Int 
817	
818	THEOREM SumFunctionEmpty ==
819	  ASSUME NEW fun, DOMAIN fun = {}
820	  PROVE  SumFunction(fun) = 0
821	
822	THEOREM SumFunctionNonempty ==
823	  ASSUME NEW fun, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun), NEW x \in DOMAIN fun,
824	         \A i \in DOMAIN fun : fun[i] \in Int
825	  PROVE  SumFunction(fun) = fun[x] + SumFunctionOnSet(fun, (DOMAIN fun) \ {x})
826	
827	THEOREM SumFunctionExcept ==
828	  ASSUME NEW fun, NEW i, NEW y \in Int, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun),
829	         \A x \in DOMAIN fun : fun[x] \in Int 
830	  PROVE  SumFunction([fun EXCEPT ![i] = y]) =
831	         IF i \in DOMAIN fun 
832	         THEN SumFunction(fun) + y - fun[i]
833	         ELSE SumFunction(fun)
834	
835	THEOREM SumFunctionZero ==
836	  ASSUME NEW fun, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN fun), 
837	         \A x \in DOMAIN fun : fun[x] \in Nat
838	  PROVE  SumFunction(fun) = 0 <=> \A x \in DOMAIN fun : fun[x] = 0
839	
840	(*************************************************************************)
841	(* Summing a constant function yields the constant multiplied by the     *)
842	(* cardinality of its domain. For example,                               *)
843	(*   SumFunction([s \in {"a","b","c"} |-> 5]) = 15.                      *)
844	(*************************************************************************)
845	THEOREM SumFunctionConst ==
846	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW c \in Int
847	  PROVE  SumFunction([s \in S |-> c]) = c * Cardinality(S)
848	
849	THEOREM SumFunctionMonotonic ==
850	  ASSUME NEW f, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN f), NEW g, DOMAIN g = DOMAIN f,
851	         \A x \in DOMAIN f : f[x] \in Int,
852	         \A x \in DOMAIN f : g[x] \in Int,
853	         \A x \in DOMAIN f : f[x] <= g[x]
854	  PROVE  SumFunction(f) <= SumFunction(g)
855	
856	THEOREM SumFunctionStrictlyMonotonic ==
857	  ASSUME NEW f, IsFiniteSet(DOMAIN f), NEW g, DOMAIN g = DOMAIN f,
858	         \A x \in DOMAIN f : f[x] \in Int,
859	         \A x \in DOMAIN f : g[x] \in Int,
860	         \A x \in DOMAIN f : f[x] <= g[x],
861	         NEW s \in DOMAIN f, f[s] < g[s]
862	  PROVE  SumFunction(f) < SumFunction(g)
863	
864	=============================================================================
865	\* Created Thu Apr 11 10:36:10 PDT 2013 by tomr
